title: Decorative Window Shutters and Flower Boxes
summary: Hinged heart-cutout shutters and a pair of angled flower boxes — a small, glue-and-jigsaw accessory build for a playhouse, bunk bed, or shed window.
description:
This is an accessory build, not a standalone piece of furniture — a set of decorative shutters and window boxes meant to dress up a playhouse-style window, whether that's a themed kids' bunk bed, a playhouse, or a garden shed. Both parts are small and forgiving, which makes it a good project for practicing clean jigsaw curves without much material at stake if a cut goes wide.

The flower boxes have one deceptively fussy detail: the front board's bottom edge sits at a slant to match the angled sides, so it usually needs a light sanding pass to seat tight against the bottom board. The shutters are built as glued-up pairs — trace your design once, cut both halves of a pair at the same time with the boards clamped together, and the pattern lines up automatically when you glue them into one panel.

tools.note: For the arched shutter tops and the decorative cutout design.
For holding matched shutter-board pairs together while tracing and cutting.

cutList.note: Arched top cut to match the window shape, plus a decorative cutout design; glued in matched pairs into 4 finished shutters.
10° angled cut, measurement on the short side of the board.

steps.title: Build the flower box bottoms and sides
steps.body: Starting with the bottom board, attach the two angled side pieces with glue and a nailer. The angled end of each side should face the front of the box, with the longer edge on top.

steps.title: Add the back
steps.body: Slide the rear piece in between the two side pieces and glue and nail it to the sides and bottom.

steps.title: Add the front
steps.body: Glue and nail the front piece on, keeping its top edge flush with the sides. The bottom of the front piece slants inward to match the angled sides, so a light sanding pass on the bottom board may be needed for a tight fit.

steps.title: Mount the flower boxes
steps.body: Center each finished box under its window and attach to the wall with wood glue and a nailer.

steps.title: Cut the shutter cutout design
steps.body: Lay the shutter boards flat in matched pairs, aligning the edges flush. Cut a decorative design — roughly 3" — out of paper as a template, and trace it onto each pair, centering the design between the two boards so it lines up once glued. Cut along your lines with a jigsaw and sand every edge smooth.

steps.title: Cut the arched tops and glue the pairs
steps.body: With the boards still paired, lay a paper window template on top, flush to the bottom and outside edges, and trace the arch that spans across each pair. Cut the arch with a jigsaw and sand the edges. Glue each pair together into one finished shutter panel and clamp until dry, wiping away any glue squeeze-out before it sets.

steps.title: Hang the shutters
steps.body: Install a shutter on each side of the window with small decorative hinges and screws. Shim each shutter at least 1/8" above the windowsill so it doesn't scrape the sill as it opens and closes.

images.alt: Decorative arched window shutters with heart cutouts above matching angled flower boxes
